perturbation.original_text: 6. Breaches 6.1 A breach of this Confidentiality and Conflict of Interest Agreement may result in action being taken against the member (up to and including removal from the Board and/or SAMED Committee). 6.2 In the case of a breach by a Board or Committee Member, the Board will determine what steps should be taken under the circumstances (provided that consid eration of the removal of a Board member shall be on thirty (30) days notice to the Board M ember. After thirty (30) days notice to the Board M ember, the Board, may remove the person from the Board through the applicable provisions and procedures created by the SAMED Constitution.
perturbation.changed_text: 6. Breaches 6.1 A breach of this Confidentiality and Conflict of Interest Agreement may result in action being taken against the member (up to and including removal from the Board and/or SAMED Committee). 6.2 In the case of a breach by a Board or Committee Member, the Board will determine what steps should be taken under the circumstances (provided that consideration of the removal of a Board member shall be on five (5) days notice to the Board Member. After five (5) days notice to the Board Member, the Board, may remove the person from the Board through the applicable provisions and procedures created by the SAMED Constitution.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the notice period for removal of a board member to 5 days is likely inconsistent with principles of due process and fairness that are often required by law or organizational bylaws, even if South African law is applicable, such a short notice period may be deemed unreasonable.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Principles of Due Process and Fairness
perturbation.law_citation: Universal Declaration of Human Rights, Article 10
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 10 of the Universal Declaration of Human Rights states that everyone is entitled to a fair and public hearing by an independent and impartial tribunal, in the determination of his rights and obligations. While not directly legally binding in all jurisdictions, it establishes a standard of fairness. Five days' notice for potential removal does not allow sufficient time for preparation or response, thus contradicting principles of fairness. Note: As South African law is specified, South African administrative law and principles of fairness would be more directly applicable, but the UDHR serves as a general example.

Drafted by a UN committee chaired by Eleanor Roosevelt , it was accepted by the General Assembly as Resolution 217 during its third session on 10 December 1948 at the Palais de Chaillot in Paris, France. [ 1 ] Of the 58 members of the United Nations at the time, 48 voted in favour, none against, eight abstained , and two did not vote. [ 2 ] A foundational text in the history of human and civil rights , the Declaration consists of 30 articles detailing an individual's "basic rights and fundamental freedoms" and affirming their univ
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the notice period for potential removal of a board member from 30 days to 5 days. The Law Explanation highlights that Article 10 of the Universal Declaration of Human Rights establishes a standard of fairness, and five days' notice may not allow sufficient time for preparation or response. While the scraped snippet is only a Wikipedia page on the Universal Declaration of Human Rights, it states that the UDHR enshrines the 'basic rights and fundamental freedoms' of all human beings. Reducing the notice period significantly undermines the principle of fairness by not allowing adequate time for the board member to prepare a defense, thus creating a legal contradiction in terms of due process and fairness.

perturbation.original_text: 7. Terms 7.1 This agreement shall commence upon the date of signature by both parties (“the effective date") to this agreement and shall continue to bind the parties for the duration of one year . A new agreement must be signed should a person be re -elected or re - appointed as a Board - and/or Committee member.
perturbation.changed_text: 7. Terms 7.1 This agreement shall commence upon the date of signature by both parties (“the effective date") to this agreement and shall continue to bind the parties indefinitely . A new agreement must be signed should a person be re -elected or re - appointed as a Board - and/or Committee member.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the one-year term and making the agreement indefinite could create legal issues regarding enforceability. While not automatically illegal, courts may be hesitant to enforce agreements that lack a defined termination point, particularly concerning confidentiality and conflict of interest, especially where specific South African employment or contract law requires reasonable terms.
perturbation.law_citation: South African Contract Law - Requirement of Certainty
perturbation.law_explanation: South African contract law requires certainty in contract terms. Although these links do not refer to this rule directly, they do mention the rules relating to contract law in South Africa, and in general, a contract which is indefinite may be void for vagueness if it lacks mechanisms for termination or adjustment. Changing the term to 'indefinitely' removes that certainty, potentially rendering the clause unenforceable under South African law. The indefinite term makes the duration of the obligation unclear and potentially unreasonable.
perturbation.justification: The modification alters the contract duration from a fixed term (one year) to an indefinite term. The Law Explanation indicates that South African contract law requires certainty in contract terms, and an indefinite term may render the clause unenforceable. While there are no snippets, the Perturbation Explanation supports that courts may be hesitant to enforce agreements that lack a defined termination point. Since the original text specifies a one-year term, removing it creates uncertainty about the duration of the contract, potentially contradicting South African contract law principles regarding certainty, making the contradiction direct and serious.

perturbation.original_text: 5.5 In the event of a conflict of interest, the interested party shall: a) Prior to any vote on the issue, decl are the con flict of interest to the Chair or Board as a whole. b) Refrain from voting in relation to the matter. c) Withdraw from the meeting when the matter is discussed if requested to do so by a simple majority of the members present and voting at the meeting.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.5 In the event of a conflict of interest, the interested party shall: a) Prior to any vote on the issue, decl are the con flict of interest to the Chair or Board as a whole. b) May vote in relation to the matter. c) Remain in the meeting when the matter is discussed regardless if requested to do so by a simple majority of the members present and voting at the meeting.
perturbation.explanation: Allowing a conflicted party to vote and remain in the meeting, even if requested to leave, directly contradicts fundamental principles of conflict of interest management and potentially violates regulations governing board conduct, even in South Africa. Such actions could be seen as a breach of fiduciary duty.
perturbation.law_citation: South African Companies Act 71 of 2008, Section 76
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 76 of the South African Companies Act outlines the duties of directors, which include acting in good faith and with the care, skill, and diligence that can reasonably be expected of a director. While this document isn't the exact law, it does refer to it and speaks of director's duties. Allowing conflicted parties to vote undermines these duties and can expose the board to legal challenges. This secondary URL shows the same standard principle in a different locale.

perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts fundamental principles of conflict of interest management. The original text mandated that an interested party refrain from voting and potentially withdraw from the meeting when a conflict of interest arises. The changed text allows the conflicted party to vote and remain in the meeting, regardless of requests to leave. This undermines the director's duties to act in good faith, as highlighted in the Law Explanation referencing Section 76 of the South African Companies Act, suggesting a breach of fiduciary duty.

perturbation.original_text: 4. Confidentiality 4.1 Regular Board meetings are , in the ordinary course of events, not open to the public and/or media. a) Members of SAMED may on invitation attend Board meetings. b) Once the Board has dealt with an issue in a board meeting and has decided on that matter , the issue is considered to be accessible to members to the extent of that decision. Matters that are so declared may be accessible to the public at large to the exten t so declared by the Board. c) All decisions that would be accessible to the general membership and/or the public at large will be recorded as such in the Board minutes. d) If no recordal is made, the matter, discussions and all resolutions should be deemed to be confidential unless declared by the Board by resolution as not confidential.
perturbation.changed_text: 4. Confidentiality 4.1 Regular Board meetings are open to the public and/or media. a) Members of SAMED may on invitation attend Board meetings. b) Once the Board has dealt with an issue in a board meeting and has decided on that matter , the issue is considered to be accessible to members to the extent of that decision. Matters that are so declared may be accessible to the public at large to the exten t so declared by the Board. c) All decisions that would be accessible to the general membership and/or the public at large will be recorded as such in the Board minutes. d) If no recordal is made, the matter, discussions and all resolutions should be deemed to be confidential unless declared by the Board by resolution as not confidential.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the policy to make all board meetings open to the public and media, regardless of the sensitive nature of the discussions, may conflict with privacy laws or regulations related to the protection of confidential business information and trade secrets.
perturbation.law_citation: South African Trade Secrets Law (Common Law Principles)
perturbation.law_explanation: South Africa relies on common law principles to protect trade secrets. This url provides the Judgement of the case *Waste Products Utilisation CC and Another v Wilkes and Another* to show Trade Secrets laws. This Judgment shows how businesses may make a civil claim for unfair competition if a trade secret is wrongfully disclosed. Opening board meetings could lead to the disclosure of confidential information, potentially harming SAMED's members or SAMED itself and creating legal liability.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Trade secret - Wikipedia Jump to content From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia Business information kept secret to gain or maintain a competitive advantage For other uses, see Trade secret (disambiguation) . Intellectual property Authors' rights Copyleft Copyright Database right Farmers' rights Geographical indication Indigenous intellectual property Industrial design right Integrated circuit layout design protection Intellectual property infringement Literary property Moral rights Patent Peasants' rights Plant breeders' rights Plant genetic resources Proprietary software Related rights Supplementary protection certificate Trade dress Trade secret Trademark Utility model Patent infringement Related topics Abandonware Artificial intelligence and copyright Brand protection Copyright abolition Copyright troll Criticism of copyright Bioprospecting Biopiracy Idea–expression distinction Limitations and exceptions to copyright Fair dealing Fair use Paraphrasing Right to quote Orphan work Patent troll Pirate Party Public domain Outline of intellectual property Outline of patents Higher categories: Property and Property law v t e A trade secret is a form of intellectual property (IP) comprising confidential information that is not generally known or readily ascertainable, derives economic value from its secrecy, and is protected by reasonable efforts to maintain its confidentiality. [ 1 ] [ 2 ] [ 3 ] Well-known examples include the Coca-Cola formula and the recipe for Kentucky Fried Chicken . Unlike other forms of IP, trade secrets do not require formal registration and can be protected indefinitely, as long as they remain undisclosed. [ 4 ] Instead, non-disclosure agreements (NDAs), among other measures, are commonly used to keep the information secret. [ 5 ] [ 6 ] Like other IP assets, trade secrets may be sold or licensed. [ 7 ] Unauthorized acquisition, use, or disclosure of a trade secret by others in a manner contrary to honest commercial practices is considered misappropriation of the trade secret. If trade secret misappropriation happens, the trade secret holder can seek various legal remedies . [ 7 ] Definition [ edit ] The precise definition of a trade secret varies by jurisdiction , as do the types of information eligible for trade secret protection. However, in general, trade secrets are confidential information that is: not generally known among or accessible to individuals within the relevant business sector; [ 3 ] commercially valuable because it is secret; [ 3 ] and subject to reasonable steps taken by the rightful holder of the information to keep it secret. [ 3 ] The Coca-Cola formula has been a trade secret since 1891. All three elements are required. If any element ceases to exist, then the trade secret will also cease to exist. [ 4 ] Trade secret protection covers confidential information, which can include technical and scientific data, business and commercial information, and financial records. [ 3 ] Even "negative" information, like failed experiments, can be valuable by helping companies avoid repeating costly mistakes. [ 3 ] In international law, while "trade secrets" and "confidential information" are often used interchangeably, trade secrets are technically a subset of confidential information. [ 8 ] To qualify as a trade secret, confidential information must meet the specific requirements set by a country's national laws, which are often influenced by Article 39 of the TRIPS Agreement . [ 8 ] [ 9 ] History [ edit ] Early origins [ edit ] Commentators like A. Arthur Schiller have argued that trade secrets were protected under Roman law by a claim known as actio servi corrupti , meaning an "action for making a slave worse" or "an action for corrupting a servant." The Roman law is described as follows: [T]he Roman owner of a mark or firm name was legally protected against unfair usage by a competitor through the actio servi corrupti ... which the Roman jurists used to grant commercial relief under the guise of
perturbation.justification: The change from regular board meetings being 'not open to the public' to being 'open to the public' directly contradicts the principle of protecting trade secrets. The Law Explanation describes South Africa relying on common law principles to protect trade secrets. The scraped snippet on trade secrets highlights that they are 'confidential information that is not generally known' and 'protected by reasonable efforts to maintain its confidentiality.' Opening board meetings to the public increases the risk of disclosing confidential information, which conflicts with the need to protect trade secrets, creating a legal contradiction.
